questions.question: Explain the concept of a multilevel hierarchy in Object-Oriented Programming. Describe how inheritance works across multiple levels and provide a simple Java code snippet illustrating a three-level class hierarchy.
keyPoints: A multilevel hierarchy is a chain of inheritance where a subclass becomes the superclass for another subclass.
Inheritance allows the subclass to inherit the properties and methods of all its parent classes up the chain.
An example hierarchy: Class A (grandparent) -> Class B (parent) extends A -> Class C (child) extends B.

questions.question: Describe the role of the 'super' keyword in a multilevel hierarchy context. How is it used to access different levels of the inheritance chain, and what are the potential pitfalls if it is used incorrectly?
keyPoints: The 'super' keyword is used to refer to the immediate parent class object and can call its constructor or methods.
It can be chained constructors to ensure proper initialization up the entire hierarchy.
Incorrect use, like calling super in the wrong order, can lead to compilation errors or improper object state.

questions.question: What is method overriding in a multilevel hierarchy? How does the JVM determine which version of an overridden method to execute for an object?
keyPoints: Method overriding occurs when a subclass provides a specific implementation for a method already defined in its superclass.
The JVM uses dynamic method dispatch (runtime polymorphism) to determine the method to execute based on the actual object's type, not the reference type.
The most specific version of the method (the one lowest in the hierarchy) is chosen at runtime.
